timeline.event: Born to Pharaoh Sneferu and Queen Hetepheres I.
Ascended to the throne of Egypt, becoming the second pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty.
Ordered and oversaw the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza, which would be his tomb.
Dispatched an expedition to Wadi Maghareh in the Sinai Peninsula, sponsoring the mining of turquoise and copper.
Died after a reign of about 23 years, presumably interred within the Great Pyramid of Giza.

keyAchievements.title: Commissioned the Great Pyramid of Giza
keyAchievements.description: He initiated the construction of the largest pyramid in Egypt, a structure that held the record as the tallest human-made building for over 3,800 years.

keyAchievements.title: Established Centralized State Power
keyAchievements.description: The pyramid project required an unprecedented level of state control over resources, labor, and administration, demonstrating and consolidating the pharaoh's power.

keyAchievements.title: Khufu's Ship
keyAchievements.description: An intact, full-sized solar barque was discovered sealed in a pit at the foot of the Great Pyramid, intended to carry the resurrected king into the heavens.

faq.question: Was the Great Pyramid built by slaves?
faq.answer: No. Current archaeological evidence indicates the pyramid was built by skilled Egyptian citizen workers who were well-fed, housed, and worked in shifts.

faq.question: What is the only surviving statue of Khufu?
faq.answer: The only positively identified, completely three-dimensional depiction of him is a tiny 7.5 cm (3 inch) ivory figurine found at the temple of Abydos.

faq.question: How long did Khufu rule?
faq.answer: While the later historian Manetho recorded a reign of 63 years, the contemporary Turin King List papyrus gives him 23 years, which is the figure more widely accepted by modern Egyptologists.

timeline.event: Born as a member of the Ptolemaic dynasty in Alexandria, Egypt.
Ascended to the throne as co-ruler with her younger brother Ptolemy XIII following her father's death.
After being exiled from her brother's court, she met Julius Caesar and sought his help to restore her throne.
Gave birth to Caesarion, widely believed to be the son of Julius Caesar.
Following Julius Caesar's assassination in Rome, Cleopatra returned to Egypt to secure her position.
Met Mark Antony in Tarsus, initiating a legendary political and romantic alliance.
Cleopatra and Antony's forces were decisively defeated by Octavian at the Battle of Actium.
Committed suicide in Alexandria to avoid capture by Octavian, traditionally believed to be by the bite of an asp.

keyAchievements.title: Prolonged Egyptian Independence
keyAchievements.description: Through adept diplomacy and strategic alliances, she maintained Egypt's independence from Roman annexation for nearly two decades.

keyAchievements.title: Economic Management
keyAchievements.description: She stabilized the Egyptian economy, managed famines, and reformed the currency, creating a period of relative prosperity.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Integration
keyAchievements.description: She was the first Ptolemaic ruler to learn the Egyptian language, and actively participated in Egyptian religious life, presenting herself as the reincarnation of the goddess Isis.

keyAchievements.title: Polyglot and Scholar
keyAchievements.description: According to ancient sources, she spoke nine languages and was a highly educated ruler with knowledge of medicine, alchemy, and philosophy.

faq.question: Was Cleopatra as beautiful as legend suggests?
faq.answer: While Roman propaganda focused on her beauty, contemporary sources like Plutarch emphasize that her charisma, intelligence, and captivating voice were her most powerful tools of seduction rather than conventional physical beauty.

faq.question: Was Cleopatra Egyptian?
faq.answer: Ethnically, she was of Greek-Macedonian descent, like all rulers of the Ptolemaic dynasty. However, she was born in Egypt and was the only ruler of her dynasty to learn and speak the Egyptian language and embrace its culture and religion.

faq.question: How did Cleopatra actually die?
faq.answer: The most famous story is that she committed suicide by the bite of an asp. While this is the most dramatic and widely accepted version, some historians debate that she may have used a fast-acting poison instead. Her goal was to avoid being paraded as a captive in Octavian's triumph in Rome.

timeline.event: Kimpa Vita was born near Mount Kibangu in the Kingdom of Kongo.
After a severe illness, she claimed to be possessed by the spirit of Saint Anthony of Padua and began her religious movement.
Led thousands of followers (Antonianists) to reoccupy and rebuild the abandoned capital, Mbanza Kongo.
Captured by forces loyal to King Pedro IV and tried for heresy by a civil council under the influence of Capuchin missionaries.
Kimpa Vita was executed by burning at the stake along with her young son.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Antonianism
keyAchievements.description: She created a popular syncretic Christian movement that reinterpreted Christianity within a Kongolese cultural framework.

keyAchievements.title: Rebuilding the Capital
keyAchievements.description: She led thousands of followers to reoccupy and rebuild the abandoned capital of Mbanza Kongo, creating a new political center.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Resistance
keyAchievements.description: She became an enduring symbol of anti-colonial sentiment and the struggle for African political and religious autonomy.

faq.question: Was Kimpa Vita a Christian?
faq.answer: Yes, but she led the Antonian movement which reinterpreted Christian doctrines to be specifically Kongolese, challenging the teachings of European missionaries.

faq.question: Why was she executed?
faq.answer: She was condemned as a heretic by the council of the Kongolese King Pedro IV, under the influence of Capuchin missionaries, because her teachings undermined both royal and church authority.

faq.question: What is her legacy?
faq.answer: She is remembered as a nationalist figure who sought to unite her people and restore the Kingdom of Kongo, and as a religious innovator who challenged colonial religious authority.

timeline.event: Born into a Berber tribe in North Africa.
Appointed governor of Tangier by Musa ibn Nusayr.
Crossed the Strait of Gibraltar and led an army into the Iberian Peninsula.
Defeated the Visigothic King Roderic at the Battle of Guadalete.
Rapidly conquered major cities including Cordoba and the Visigothic capital, Toledo.
Recalled to Damascus by Caliph Al-Walid I along with Musa ibn Nusayr.
Died in obscurity in Damascus.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Hispania
keyAchievements.description: Led the initial Umayyad army that conquered most of the Iberian Peninsula from the Visigoths, initiating centuries of Islamic rule.

keyAchievements.title: Battle of Guadalete
keyAchievements.description: Achieved a decisive victory against a numerically superior Visigothic army, resulting in the collapse of their kingdom.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of Gibraltar
keyAchievements.description: The name 'Gibraltar' is a Spanish derivation from the Arabic 'Jabal Ṭāriq', meaning 'Mountain of Tariq'.

faq.question: Was Tariq ibn Ziyad Arab or Berber?
faq.answer: He was a Berber of North African tribal origin. His army was also predominantly composed of Berbers.

faq.question: Did he really burn his ships?
faq.answer: This is a famous story, but most modern historians consider it a later legend meant to emphasize his point of no return. The earliest Arabic accounts of the conquest make no mention of it.

faq.question: Why did he fall from grace?
faq.answer: His superior, Musa ibn Nusayr, reportedly became jealous of his success. Both were recalled to Damascus by the Caliph, who politically feared they were becoming too powerful, and Tariq was never given a command again.

timeline.event: Born into an aristocratic family in the Semien province of Ethiopia.
Married Sahle Maryam of Shewa, the future Emperor Menelik II.
Selected the site and named Addis Ababa ('New Flower'), which would become the future capital of Ethiopia.
Became Empress of Ethiopia upon Menelik II's coronation. She grew suspicious of the Italian version of the Treaty of Wuchale.
Led her own forces and advised Menelik II at the Battle of Adwa, playing a decisive strategic role that led to Ethiopia's decisive victory over Italy.
Emperor Menelik II died. Taytu was pushed out of power by court rivals who feared her influence.
Died in Entoto, confined to an old palace.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Adwa
keyAchievements.description: Her strategic advice and military leadership played a decisive role in Ethiopia defeating the Italian army and preserving the nation's independence.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Addis Ababa
keyAchievements.description: She chose the location that would become the capital of Ethiopia and gave it the name 'Addis Ababa' (New Flower).

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Resistance
keyAchievements.description: She was the first to recognize the deceptive nature of the Treaty of Wuchale and strongly advocated for its nullification.

faq.question: Why is Taytu Betul so important in Ethiopian history?
faq.answer: She was a powerful Empress who effectively co-ruled with her husband Menelik II. Her political and military genius were crucial in defending Ethiopia from Italian colonization, especially at the Battle of Adwa, making Ethiopia a symbol of African independence.

faq.question: What does 'Taytu' mean?
faq.answer: Her name Taytu (or Taitu) means 'Sun'. Because of her brilliance and powerful presence, she was often referred to as the 'Sun of Ethiopia'.

faq.question: What happened to her after the death of Emperor Menelik II?
faq.answer: Following Menelik's death in 1913, political rivals who feared her influence conspired to remove her from power. She was confined to the old palace at Entoto, where she lived until her death in 1918.
